title: Multiple Accounts Confusion
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: How does simplifying your banking accounts improve financial control?
What are the risks of maintaining multiple unused accounts?

stages.prompt: You have three bank accounts you rarely use. What's better?

options.label: Keep all active
options.reflection: Maintaining multiple unused accounts can lead to confusion, fees, and difficulty tracking your finances.

options.label: Use one or two actively and close unused ones
options.reflection: Exactly! Simplifying to fewer active accounts improves control and reduces errors in tracking your finances.

options.label: Open even more accounts for different purposes
options.reflection: Opening more accounts would increase complexity rather than solving the problem of managing multiple accounts.

options.label: Ignore them and let them accumulate fees
options.reflection: Ignoring accounts can lead to unwanted fees and potentially negative balances, which is not ideal.

stages.prompt: What is a potential problem with having too many bank accounts?

options.label: More opportunities to save money
options.reflection: While having more accounts might seem like more saving opportunities, it often leads to confusion and poor oversight.

options.label: Better interest rates
options.reflection: Having more accounts doesn't necessarily mean better interest rates; it just adds complexity.

options.label: Increased security
options.reflection: More accounts don't increase security; they can actually make it harder to monitor for fraudulent activity.

options.label: Difficulty tracking balances across accounts
options.reflection: Exactly! Having multiple accounts can make it challenging to monitor your total financial position.

stages.prompt: How does consolidating bank accounts help with budgeting?

options.label: Makes budgeting more difficult
options.reflection: Actually, fewer accounts make budgeting easier by simplifying tracking of income and expenses.

options.label: Allows for complete separation of funds
options.reflection: While accounts can be used for separate purposes, too many accounts complicate rather than simplify tracking.

options.label: Makes it simpler to track income and expenses
options.reflection: Exactly! Fewer accounts mean you have fewer places to track money movements, simplifying budgeting.

options.label: Automatically manages your money
options.reflection: Consolidating accounts simplifies tracking but doesn't automatically manage your money.

stages.prompt: What is a benefit of having fewer bank accounts?

options.label: Creates more complex financial tracking
options.reflection: Fewer accounts actually simplify financial tracking rather than making it more complex.

options.label: Reduces the chance of errors in financial management
options.reflection: Exactly! Fewer accounts mean less chance of making errors like depositing to the wrong account.

options.label: Requires higher minimum balances
options.reflection: Having fewer accounts doesn't require higher minimum balances across all accounts.

options.label: Increases overall banking fees
options.reflection: Fewer accounts often mean fewer fees, especially if you're paying maintenance fees per account.

stages.prompt: When should you consider closing an unused bank account?

options.label: When it has been inactive for a long time
options.reflection: Exactly! Closing inactive accounts reduces complexity and eliminates potential fees for unused services.

options.label: Never close any account
options.reflection: Keeping unused accounts indefinitely can lead to fees and complications in financial management.

options.label: Only when it has zero balance
options.reflection: While zero balance is important, you can transfer funds and close accounts that have been inactive for long periods.

options.label: Only when opening a new account
options.reflection: Closing accounts should be based on their usefulness rather than tied to opening new ones.
